coredata.dc:title: A real-time fluorescent assay for the detection of alkaline phosphatase activity based on carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Qian, Zhao Sheng
Chai, Lu Jing
Huang, Yuan Yuan
Tang, Cong
Jia Shen, Jia
Chen, Jian Rong
Feng, Hui
coredata.dc:description: A convenient and real-time fluorometric assay with the assistance of copper ions based on aggregation and disaggregation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was developed to achieve highly sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase activity. CQDs and pyrophosphate anions (PPi) were used as the fluorescent indicator and substrate for ALP activity assessment respectively. Richness of carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs enables their severe aggregation triggered by copper ions, which results in effective fluorescence quenching. Under the catalytic hydrolysis of ALP, PPi can be rapidly transformed to phosphate ions. Stronger affinity of phosphate ions to copper ions than carboxyl groups is taken advantage of to achieve fluorescence recovery induced by re-dispersion of CQDs in the presence of ALP and PPi. Quantitative evaluation of ALP activity in a broad range from 16.7 to 782.6U/L with the detection limit of 1.1U/L can be realized in this way, which endows the assay with high enough sensitivity for practical detection in human serum. This strategy broadens the sensing application of fluorescent CQDs with excellent biocompatibility, and provides an example based on disaggregation in optical probe development.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity
Real-time fluorometric assay
Aggregation and disaggregation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of quantum yield of CQDs
Quantitative determination of PPi based on CQDs
Respective selectivity of the sensing system towards Cu2+ and PPi
Principle of ALP level detection based on CQDs and PPi
Assessment of detection strategy for PPi based on CQDs and Cu2+
Assessment of detection strategy for ALP activity based on CQDs and PPi

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of original CQDs, (B) aggregates of CQDs and copper ions, (C) re-dispersed CQDs in the presence of copper ions and PPi, and (D) re-aggregated CQDs with copper ions in the presence of PPi and ALP.

(A) The changes of fluorescence spectra of CQDs in the presence of different composition: (a) mere CQDs; (b) in the presence of Cu2+ (100.0µM); (c) in the presence of Cu2+ (100.0µM) and PPi (167.0µM); and (d) in the presence of Cu2+ (100.0µM), PPi (167.0µM) and ALP (6111 U/L). (B) The changes of fluorescence spectra of CQDs with varying amounts of Cu2+. (C) The changes of fluorescence spectra of CQDs and Cu2+ mixture with increasing concentrations of PPi. (D) Relative fluorescence intensity (I−I 0)/I 0 of the sensing system in the presence of different anions. The concentration of each anion is 83.3µM.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of sensing system containing CQDs solution, Cu2+ (100.0µM), PPi (167.0µM) and ALP (1.67U/mL) as lengthening the incubation time from 0 to 120min. (B) The fluorescence intensity of the assay with different units (0–5045U/L) of ALP activity within 30min incubation time. Inset: The linear fitting curve between fluorescence intensity and log value of ALP level.
Schematic illustration of the detection strategy for PPi and ALP activity based on aggregation and disaggregation of carbon quantum dots.

head.ce:title: A real-time fluorescent assay for the detection of alkaline phosphatase activity based on carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua 321004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A convenient and real-time fluorometric assay with the assistance of copper ions based on aggregation and disaggregation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was developed to achieve highly sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase activity. CQDs and pyrophosphate anions (PPi) were used as the fluorescent indicator and substrate for ALP activity assessment respectively. Richness of carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs enables their severe aggregation triggered by copper ions, which results in effective fluorescence quenching. Under the catalytic hydrolysis of ALP, PPi can be rapidly transformed to phosphate ions. Stronger affinity of phosphate ions to copper ions than carboxyl groups is taken advantage of to achieve fluorescence recovery induced by re-dispersion of CQDs in the presence of ALP and PPi. Quantitative evaluation of ALP activity in a broad range from 16.7 to 782.6U/L with the detection limit of 1.1U/L can be realized in this way, which endows the assay with high enough sensitivity for practical detection in human serum. This strategy broadens the sensing application of fluorescent CQDs with excellent biocompatibility, and provides an example based on disaggregation in optical probe development.

ce:section.ce:para: Phosphate species widely present in biological system play vital roles in many important biological processes. As a hydrolysis product of adenosine triphosphate (ATP), pyrophosphate anions (PPi) have caused particularly much attention not only because they involve in DNA polymerization (Ronaghi et al., 1996), enzymatic reactions (Steinberg et al., 2008) and cellular metabolism (Farre et al., 2000), but also are related to some severe medical conditions in term of their levels (Kim et al., 2009). It is reported that the detection of PPi has become an important issue in cancer research and it is essential to develop facile method for PPi monitoring in aqueous physiological system (Xu et al., 2001). In addition, the hydrolysis of PPi by alkaline phosphatase (ALP) plays a crucial role in skeletal mineralization and vascular calcification, thus the analysis of ALP using PPi as its natural substrate is of great significance (Liu et al., 2014a, 2014b). Furthermore, as the most common human alkaline phosphatase, ALP is often used as a key biomarker in diagnosis of many diseases. The abnormal level of ALP in serum is closely related to various diseases including breast and prostatic cancer (Lorente et al., 1999), bone disease (Colornbatto et al., 1996a), liver dysfunction and diabetes (Colornbatto et al., 1996b). Therefore, there is an urgent demand for convenient and sensitive methods for real-time ALP activity monitoring.

Up to date, various fluorescent chemosensors for detection of PPi and/or ALP have been reported using organic dyes (Zhu et al., 2012a, 2012b; Feng et al., 2012; Su et al., 2013), inorganic semiconductor dots (QDs) (Du et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2014a, 2014b) or noble metal nanoclusters (Deng et al., 2013a, 2013b; Sun et al., 2014; Kim et al., 2013; Chen et al., 2014) as fluorometric indicators. However, it has been shown that these fluorophores suffer their own non-negligible drawbacks such as poor photostability for dyes, high toxicity for QDs, and high cost and poor stability in aqueous system for noble metal nanoclusters. Stability, toxicity and cost of the used materials in the chemosensor or biosensor constitute important concerns for their universal and practical application, especially for implantable biosensors. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a new fluorescent nanomaterial possess much superiority including good photostability, excellent biocompatibility, cheap raw materials and outstanding water solubility (Baker and Baker, 2010; Shen et al., 2012; Li et al., 2013a, 2013b; Zhu et al., 2012a, 2012b), and thus endow them as the best alternative of fluorometric indicator to practical application in chemosensor and biosensor. Our group have shown that CQDs as an excellent fluorophore can be used to achieve respective and simultaneous detection of DNA and/or thrombin with high selectivity and sensitivity (Qian et al., 2014a, 2014b, 2014c).

In this communication, a convenient and highly sensitive fluorescence sensing approach for PPi detection and ALP activity monitoring based on aggregation and disaggregation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with the assistance of copper ions was developed. We take advantage of good biocompatibility and strong fluorescence of CQDs, unique aggregation of CQDs caused by copper ions, effective disaggregation induced by competitive interaction of PPi to copper ions, and hydrolysis ability of ALP to PPi to construct the dual-functional biosensor. Scheme 1 shows the schematic illustration of detection strategy for PPi and ALP based on turn-on and turn-off processes induced by aggregation and disaggregation of CQDs with the assistance of copper ions. Because carboxylate groups on the surface of CQDs are good ligands for copper ions, copper ions were used as molecular glues to aggregate CQDs. The presence of copper ions triggers severe aggregation of CQDs due to rapid complexation between copper ions and CQDs, and thus quenches the fluorescence of CQDs. Because of stronger affinity of PPi to copper ions than carboxylate, the PPi will grab copper ions from CQDs–Cu aggregates to form preferential PPi–Cu complex. Thus, the introduction of PPi into CQD–Cu aggregate system would break up the aggregates and form more stable PPi–Cu complex and free CQDs, which leads to gradual fluorescence recovery of CQDs with the continuous addition of PPi. For further monitoring of ALP activity, various amounts of ALP are introduced into the above system. The PPi is hydrolyzed to phosphate ions by ALP, and then copper ions are released from PPi–Cu complex into the solution. The free copper ions combine with CQDs and aggregate them again, which results in another cycle of fluorescence quenching of CQDs. In a certain time, varying amounts of ALP would lead to different degrees of fluorescence quenching, which can be used to assess the activity of ALP.

Triple-distilled water was used throughout the experimental process. Activated carbon, CuSO4, pyrophosphate (PPi) and other salts were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The alkaline phosphatase (ALP, EC 3.1.3.1, 10kU) and newborn calf serum (bovine serum) were purchased from Sigma (U.S.A.) and Shenggong Company (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and without any further purification.

Activated carbon (2.0g) was added into a mixture of concentrated sulfuric acid (180ml) and nitric acid (60ml). The mixture was heated at 80°C for 5h. The mixture was cooled and diluted with deionized water (800ml). The dark CQDs solution was neutralized with sodium hydroxide. The final product solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000Da) for 3 days, and then the resultant solution was further treated with the dialysis bag (50,000Da) to remove large non-fluorescent materials. The outside solution of the bag was collected and concentrated for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of quantum yield of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The determination of quantum yield of CQDs was achieved by comparison of the wavelength integrated intensity of the samples to that of standard Rodamine 6G. The optical density was kept below 0.05 to avoid inner filter effects. The quantum yield of the CQDs was calculated using
Φ =
Φ
s
[
( I
A
s
n
2
)
/
(
I s
A
n s
2
)
]
where Φ is the quantum yield, I is the integrated intensity, A is the optical density and n is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript s refers to the standard reference of known quantum yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative determination of PPi based on CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: For optimization of incubation time for fluorescence quenching by copper ions, 30.0µL of copper solution (10.0mM) was added into 3.0mL of CQDs solution (3.5mg/mL). Then, the fluorescence of mixed solution was recorded after different shaking time of 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5min. For optimization of incubation time for fluorescence recovery by PPi, the fluorescence of CQDs was first quenched by adding 30.0µL of copper solution (10.0mM) into 3.0mL of CQDs solution. Then, 30.0µL of PPi solution (10.0mM) was introduced to the above mixture. The fluorescence was recorded after different shaking time of 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5min. For quantitative determination of PPi, 20.0µL of copper ion (10.0mM) was first added into 3.0ml of CQDs solution (3.5mg/mL) to quench the fluorescence. A certain different concentration of PPi was added into the above mixture. After 3min incubation time, the fluorescence spectra were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 452nm. The concentrations of PPi were 3.3, 6.6, 13.2, 19.8, 33.0, 46.2, 59.4, 72.6, 85.5, 112.2, 138.6 and 191.4µM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Respective selectivity of the sensing system towards Cu2+ and PPi
ce:section.ce:para: For the selectivity test towards Cu2+, twelve metal cations including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Co2+, Fe2+ and Cu2+ were chosen to evaluate the influence of metal ions on the fluorescence of CQDs and assess the selectivity towards copper ion. A certain amount of each metal cation was added into 3.00ml of CQDs solution (3.5mg/mL), where the concentration of metal cation was 100.0µM. After five minutes incubation time, the fluorescence spectra of resulting mixtures were monitored at the excitation wavelength of 452nm. For the selectivity assessment to PPi, nine kinds of different anions including F–, Cl–, Br–, I–, SO4 2–, NO3 –, Ac–, HCO3 – and PO4 3– were selected to evaluate the influence of anions on the fluorescence of CQDs–Cu system. A certain amount of copper ions were added into 3.00ml of CQDs solution (3.5mg/mL), where the concentration of copper ions was 33.3µM. A certain amount of each anion (83.3µM) was then introduced into the above CQDs–Cu system, and then fluorescence spectra of resulting mixtures were monitored at the excitation wavelength of 452nm. Tris–HCl butter (pH 7.4) was selected as the butter for selectivity assessment experiments.

The ALP activity was first assessed as extending incubation time with a certain amount of ALP level. The sensing system containing CQDs solution (3.5mg/mL), Cu2+ (100.0µM), PPi (167.0µM), Mg2+ (0.1µM) and ALP (1.67U/mL) was monitored by fluorescence spectroscopy every 5min. The fluorescence spectra of the system were recorded from 0 to 120min after the addition of ALP at the excitation wavelength of 452nm. For the real-time enzyme assay, different levels of ALP with different activity ranging from 16.7 to 5045.0U/L was respectively added into 3.00mL of CQDs solution (3.5mg/mL) containing Cu2+ (100.0µM), PPi (100.0µM) and Mg2+ (0.1µM). After an incubation time of 30min, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture were recorded respectively.

The morphologies of all samples were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), which was performed on a JEOL-2100F instrument with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. Samples were prepared by dropping aqueous suspensions of the separated fractions of samples onto Cu TEM grids coated with a holey amorphous carbon film and following solvent evaporation in a dust protected atmosphere. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analyses were conducted using a Kratos Axis ULTRA X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with a 165nm hemisphereical electron energy analyzer. The incident radiation came from monochromatic Al X-ray (1486.6eV) at 15kV and 3mA. Atomic concentrations were calculated using Vision software and a Shirley baseline. The UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Perkin Elmer Lambda 950 spectrometer. The photoluminescence spectra were conducted on a PerkinElmer LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer, and lifetimes were determined using a FLS920 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The CQDs were synthesized by chemical oxidation of activated carbon using concentrated sulfuric acid and nitric acid. Chemical oxidation was chosen as the preparation method in this work because it could introduce a great deal of oxygen-containing groups including hydroxyl and carboxyl, which endow as-prepared CQDs excellent aqueous solubility and functional groups on the surface. The as-prepared CQDs can exhibit strong yellowish green fluorescence, and are highly soluble in water and most polar organic solvents. TEM image in Fig. 1A displays CQDs have a relative narrow size distribution ranging from 3 to 5nm. In order to analyze the composition of CQDs, XPS spectra of CQDs were recorded (Fig. S1). The C1s spectrum demonstrates two distinct peaks at 284.8 and 288.6eV, which can be attributed to C=C, C–O and C=O, respectively. The elemental analysis of CQDs indicates that the CQDs are mainly composed of oxygen (25.5at%) and carbon (74.5at%). These results indicated that a great deal of oxygen-containing groups including hydroxyl and carboxyl functionals were produced on the surface of CQDs during the process of oxidation, which contributes to excellent water solubility of as-prepared CQDs. The as-synthesized CQDs show strong green photoluminescence in water under the UV lamp, and exhibit fluorescence emission band centered at 518nm at the excitation wavelength of 446nm (Fig. S2A). The corresponding emission spectra of CQDs under different excitation wavelengths in Fig. S2B shows that the peak of emission spectra are nearly not shifted as the change of the excitation wavelength, which suggests good monodispersion of CQDs in size and fluorescence, and provides an distinctive advantage over other carbon dots with shifting emission for analytical application. The quantum yield of CQDs was determined as 0.022, and the lifetime was calculated to be 6.2ns, which shows similar photoluminescence property to CQDs reported previously (Qian et al., 2014a, 2014b, 2014c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of ALP level detection based on CQDs and PPi
ce:section.ce:para: Since it was reported that copper ions can be used to aggregate molecular indicators to form chemosensing ensemble (Hortala et al., 2003; Fu et al., 2005), we assessed the influence of twelve metal cations including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Co2+, Fe2+ and Cu2+ on fluorescence of CQDs in this work. As shown in Fig. S3, the presence of Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Al3+ and Zn2+ shows little influence on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs, whereas the introduction of Fe3+, Ni2+, Co2+, Fe2+ and Cu2+ brings apparent impact to the fluorescence intensity with distinct quenching effect. Among these metal cations, copper ion has strongest quenching ability to the fluorescence of CQDs, and thus was chosen to quench the fluorescence of CQDs by possible aggregation of CQDs. From Fig. 2A, we found that the presence of copper ions caused nearly complete fluorescence quenching of CQDs. Therefore, it is reasonable to assume that cooper ions can easily bind to the surface of CQDs through carboxyl group, and the coordination of one copper ion with several CQDs can result in the severe aggregation of CQDs with each other, resulting in the reduction of the fluorescence. In order to verify the proposed mechanism of aggregation caused fluorescence quenching by copper ions, we compared TEM image of the mixture of CQDs and copper ions with that of single CQDs as shown in Fig. 1A and B. It is clearly illustrated that the CQDs are well-dispersed in the absence of copper ions, but large aggregates of CQDs with more than 10nm in diameter form in the presence of copper ions, which provided the solid evidence for the aggregation of CQDs by copper ions and resulting fluorescence quenching of CQDs, i.e., aggregation caused fluorescence quenching mechanism. Furthermore, we observed that continuous addition of PPi anions into the mixture of CQDs and Cu2+ lead to fluorescence recovery to a large extent, and the best recovery ratio is up to 80%. We speculated that the PPi would complex preferentially to copper ions and thus grab copper ions from the aggregates between CQDs and copper ions due to stronger affinity of the PPi to copper ions than the carboxylate groups, and the formation of PPi–Cu complexes would disaggregate the CQDs aggregates and set them free again, which would lead to the fluorescence recovery or enhancement with the presence of PPi. The disaggregation of CQDs caused by PPi was confirmed by TEM image in Fig. 1C, where CQDs were in the dispersion state after the addition of PPi anions. Finally, a certain amount of ALP was introduced into the mixture including CQDs, Cu2+ and PPi. After 20min incubation, it was observed that the fluorescence intensity decreased to less than half of the original value, indicating that PPi was hydrolyzed into phosphate anions under the catalysis of ALP. The free copper ions from breakdown of PPi–Cu complex re-aggregate the CQDs in the solution. This re-aggregation of CQDs in the presence of ALP was also verified by TEM image in Fig. 1D. It is suggested that ALP activity or amount can be assessed from quenched fluorescence intensity in this way.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of detection strategy for PPi based on CQDs and Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: We further evaluated the feasibility of CQDs as a fluorescent sensing platform for PPi detection and ALP activity assessment with the assistance of copper ions. Quantitative detection of PPi and ALP were performed based on the “off–on–off” fluorescence. By employment of disaggregation induced enhancement (DIE) followed aggregation caused quenching (ACQ), the rapid and sensitive determination of PPi can be easily realized. The condition optimization including the added amount of copper ions, and the incubation time for ACQ and DIE were first carried out. Fig. 2B shows gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity with the increase of copper ions in amount, and the fluorescence intensity reaches the minimum when the concentration of copper ions is up to 102.3μM. Fig. S4 displays time dependence of fluorescence intensity of the mixture of CQDs and Cu2+, and time dependence of the mixture of CQDs, Cu2+ and PPi. Both experiments showed that the fluorescence intensity remains stable when the incubation time is over two minutes, indicating the fast response time for the constructed sensor. Thus, the concentration of 66.7μM for copper ions and 3min incubation time were chosen as the optimal conditions for the following quantitative determination.

Under the optimal conditions, the sensing performance of this system for detection of PPi was evaluated by adding varying concentrations of target into the proceeding CQDs/Cu2+ mixture. The fluorescence response of these systems was recorded by adding different concentrations of PPi in pH 7.4 buffer solutions at room temperature. It can be seen from Fig. 2C that the fluorescence intensity of the system could be gradually recovered upon the addition of different concentrations of PPi. The fluorescence intensity of the system reaches the top when the concentration of added PPi is up to 191.4μM. A good linear fitting curve between fluorescence intensity and concentration of PPi can be obtained in the range of 3.3–85.8μM (Fig. S5). This equation can be expressed as y=2.46x+14.12 where R 2=0.995. The detection limit for PPi was determined to be 0.3µM as estimated form the derived calibration curve (≥3 standard deviations). To investigate the selectivity of our method, different kinds of anions that were considered to possibly interfere with the PPi sensing were added into the aqueous dispersion of CQDs containing copper ions, and the fluorescence response was recorded. As illustrated in Fig. 2D, the separate addition of each kinds of anion including F–, Cl–, Br–, I–, SO4 2–, NO3 –, HCO3 –, Ac– and PO4 3– into the aqueous dispersion of CQDs containing copper ions did not result in an obvious change in fluorescence intensity. The addition of F–, Cl–, Br–, I– and NO3 – did not induce the increase of fluorescence intensity, but caused a little quenching effect. The presence of PO4 3– leads to an obvious enhancement of fluorescence due to its ability to form Cu–PO4 complex. However, compared with the sharp increase of fluorescence by PPi, the influence of phosphate anion can be considered to be acceptable because the selectivity value (I−I 0)/I 0 for PPi is up to 7 while the value for phosphate is less than 2. These results substantially suggest that these anions did not interfere with the PPi sensing with our method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of detection strategy for ALP activity based on CQDs and PPi
ce:section.ce:para: The activity of ALP was tested in a real-time assay using CQDs aqueous solution containing Cu2+, PPi, Ca2+ and Mg2+ as the standard assay solution. The reactions were monitored by fluorescence emission spectroscopy after the addition of 1.67U/mL of ALP at room temperature. The typical fluorescence spectra were consecutively recorded every 5min as shown in Fig. 3A. The addition of ALP triggers the hydrolysis of PPi into Pi, and the breakdown of more and more PPi leads to re-aggregation of CQDs induced by more and more free copper ions as the increase of time in the presence of ALP. The gradual fluorescence quenching due to catalysis activity of ALP for hydrolysis of PPi as extending the incubation time can be observed. By compromising the quenching effect and time consumption, 30min incubation time was chosen for quantitative determination of ALP activity. The reactions were further monitored by fluorescence emission spectroscopy after addition of different units (0–5045U/L) of ALP activity after 30min incubation time. As shown in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence intensity for the standard assay solution decreased at different rate with the log value of ALP activity per liter ranging from 16.7 to 5045U/L. To obtain the relationship between the fluorescence of the assay and the activity of ALP, the fluorescence intensity is plotted as a function of log value of ALP activity. The result shows the linear scope for fitting curve between fluorescence intensity and log value of ALP activity is from 16.7 to 782.6U/L. The fitting equation can be expressed as y=−20.79x+312.00 with R 2=0.97. The detection limit based on three standard errors is estimated as 1.1U/L. Thus, the as-established real-time assay is capable of practical detection of ALP level in human body, because the normal value for which is reported to be 40–170U/L (Vella et al., 2012) or 30–120U/L (Li et al., 2013a, 2013b) in blood. This detection limit is also comparable to those based on Au nanoclusters (0.1–10U/L) reported recently (Li et al., 2013a, 2013b; Sun et al., 2014).

In order to test the practicability of this ALP activity assay in real serum sample, we assessed analytical performance of this assay in 100-fold bovine serum dilution, and compared it with that in Tris–HCl buffer. Fig. S6 shows that quenching effect of copper ion on fluorescence of CQDs is slightly impacted in the bovine serum dilution with respect to that in Tris–HCl buffer, but the performance on fluorescence recovery caused by PPi anion and following fluorescence decrease induced by ALP in bovine serum dilution is comparable to that in Tris–HCl buffer. These results indicate that the presence of bovine serum could bring some influence on fluorescence quenching ability of copper ion, but this influence is small and could be compensated by adding a little more copper ion into the system. More importantly, apparent fluorescence increase by introducing a certain amount of PPi anion into CQDs–Cu system and following gradual fluorescence decrease induced by added ALP in bovine serum dilution were also observed, suggesting that it is feasible and practical to achieve the evaluation of ALP activity in bovine serum dilution.

In summary, a label-free real-time fluorometric assay was designed for the highly sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase based on aggregation and disaggregation of CQDs with the assistance copper ions. Richness of carboxyl groups on the surface of carbon quantum dots enables aggregation caused fluorescence quenching by copper ions, and the competitive interaction among carboxyl, copper ions and PPi endows disaggregation induced fluorescence enhancement, the hydrolysis of PPi with ALP released copper ions lead to aggregation of CQDs and quenching fluorescence again. Based on this special fluorescence off–on–off process induced by aggregation–dispersion–aggregation of carbon quantum dots, rapid and effective assay for detection of PPi and ALP level are constructed. To our best knowledge, the present study is the first analytical system of a fluorescent carbon quantum dots sensor that can quantitatively and sensitively detect ALP activity in real time with a detection limit of 1.1U/L, which is sensitive enough for practical determination of ALP level in human serum. Since the materials involved in the sensing assay are of excellent biocompatibility, it is expected that as-established detection assay for ALP activity/level would be used in vivo and in vitro, and promote the application of carbon-based nanomaterials in biosensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are thankful for the support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21405142, 21005073 and 21275131) and Zhejiang Province (Nos. LY13B050001, LQ13B050002 and ZJHX201403).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2015.01.068.
